The tool query_db was executed successfully. The result is stored under key: var_functions.query_db:16 The result is: [{"rating": "4", "title": "Ha! On me! I thought this was a cookbook!", "text": "Lucky for me it's more than a cookbook.

The story of a modern Daniel Boone, growing up in Michigan, one of three sons of a hunter. Beginning at age eight when he shot a squirrel, to age 13 his first deer, then hunting his way through college, a career as a fur trapper, hunting sheep in Alaska, canoeing the Missouri River for deer and ending in Brooklyn age 37. Brooklyn?

I especially liked that I share his disgust at catch & release fishing - if you fish it, you have to eat it - anything else is sadism (my words).

My brother in law is the cook in the family and I wanted to see if this would be a good gift for him and I struck pay dirt following each chapter are \"Tasting Notes\" where the author speaks of cooking wild game.

I also enjoyed it for the history of the land he has hunted as well as the history of the hunt (I too was a big Daniel Boone fan growing up!).

I think more pictures (everything but dead things) would have made this a keeper.", "review_time": "2012-11-24 18:52:00", "helpful_vote": "0", "verified_purchase": "0", "purchase_id": "purchaseid_186"}, {"rating": "4", "title": "Four Stars", "text": "Not as developed as Stephanie but I like the characters so far.", "review_time": "2015-12-31 13:35:00", "helpful_vote": "0", "verified_purchase": "1", "purchase_id": "purchaseid_191"}, {"rating": "4", "title": "A wonderful adventure in France", "text": "I loved this book all the way until the end. I have recently discovered that the author is intending to release another book, and from what I understand it will continue where this one left off. I am thankful for this, as the way this book ended was definitely a negative for me. I wanted to know more! What happened to the author, did she learn what she came to learn? Did she get the job she was hoping for? So many questions.

From the beginning of Linda's book, it is easy to love her. She is open, honest and definitely has the type of personality you want your heroine to have, whether the book is fiction or reality. I couldn't help but root for Linda throughout her trials with her host family, even when I thought she acted as a bit of a brat herself. Listening to her internal thoughts about what she went through and her desire to achieve her goals made this book feel close to my heart.

I applaud the adventurous spirit of the author and her decision to keep journal entries from that chapter of her life. What great material to have later to inspire a book! While I enjoyed the entire memoir, my favourite part of this book would have to be the author's descriptions of the many places she visited and the people she met along the way. While I think she could definitely have made a better impression on the family she worked for if she had been honest about her lack of French language skills from the beginning, she is a pioneer. Her drive and desire to learn the language from those in the actual country was inspiring. Not many people would have been gutsy enough to do what Linda did.

I also particularly enjoyed the relationship between Linda and Antoine. The Kind heart of the author was apparent in her actions toward the children, even when she struggled with them.

There is some romance, and I appreciated the way the author handled it. While sex scenes are not necessarily automatically offensive, there is something about memoirs that makes me uncomfortable if they are filled with them. This author manages to hint at her experiences without going into too much detail, leaving the reader to decide exactly what happened. Great writing.

When you step into reading this book, you learn a lot about French customs, French cuisine and wine. You also get the opportunity to brush up on your French skills a bit and get to step outside yourself and live an adventure through the eyes of the author. I read this book in one sitting with very few breaks in between and found myself enthralled in the sights, sounds and atmosphere of this memoir.

In the end, I was disappointed that the book ended. I could have kept reading about Linda's experiences without stopping anytime in the foreseeable future. The end of the book leaves many unanswered questions, that I am assuming the author will be answering in her next book. Were this a standalone, I would be very disappointed with the way the book ended, but with the knowledge that she is writing another book to continue with her story, I can't fault this one. One thing is for sure, if you read this book, you will be anxiously awaiting the next.

I would recommend this to anyone who likes books about travel, memoirs or just a good story that came from the recollections and heart of the author. Read it, you will be glad you did. Overall, this was a VERY enjoyable read that gave me many reasons to wish I could drop everything and head off to France!", "review_time": "2013-05-05 10:47:00", "helpful_vote": "1", "verified_purchase": "0", "purchase_id": "purchaseid_190"}]

# The variable should exist in locals()

    print("Found variable with prefix")
    file_path = locals()['var_functions.query_db:20']

    available_vars = [k for k in locals().keys() if 'query_db' in str(k)]
    print(f"Available query_db variables: {available_vars}")
    
    # Try common pattern
    for k in list(locals().keys()):
        if 'query_db' in str(k) or k.endswith('20'):
            print(f"Trying key: {k}")
            # This might be the file path
            file_path = locals().get(k)

                print(f"Found file path: {file_path}")
                break

print("Reading books data...")"}

{"code": "import json
import re
import pandas as pd

# Get the file paths
books_file = locals().get('var_functions.query_db:28')
reviews_file = locals().get('var_functions.query_db:26')

print(f"Books file: {books_file}")
print(f"Reviews file: {reviews_file}")

# Load books data
with open(books_file, 'r') as f:
    books_data = json.load(f)

# Load reviews data
with open(reviews_file, 'r') as f:
    reviews_data = json.load(f)

print(f"Total books: {len(books_data)}")
print(f"Total reviews: {len(reviews_data)}")

# Sample of data
print("Sample book:", books_data[0])
print("Sample review:", reviews_data[0])

# Initialize result dict
result = {
    "books_count": len(books_data),
    "reviews_count": len(reviews_data),
    "sample_book": books_data[0],
    "sample_review": reviews_data[0]
}

print("__RESULT__:")
print(json.dumps(result))"}

function.arguments:
{"code": "import json
import re
import pandas as pd

# Load data
books_file = locals().get('var_functions.query_db:28')
reviews_file = locals().get('var_functions.query_db:26')

with open(books_file, 'r') as f:
    books = json.load(f)
with open(reviews_file, 'r') as f:
    reviews = json.load(f)

# Helper to extract numeric ID
extract_number = lambda s: re.search(r'(\d+)', s).group(1) if re.search(r'(\d+)', s) else None

# Process books: add numeric id and year
for book in books:
    book['id_num'] = extract_number(book['book_id'])
    # Extract year from details and subtitle
    text = f"{book.get('details', '')} {book.get('subtitle', '')}"
    # Find 4-digit years between 1900-2023
    years = re.findall(r'(\b(19|20)\d{2}\b)', text)
    year = None
    if years:
        year = int(years[0][0])
    book['year'] = year
    # Compute decade
    if year:
        decade = f"{year//10*10}s"
    else:
        decade = None
    book['decade'] = decade

# Process reviews: add numeric id
for rev in reviews:
    rev['id_num'] = extract_number(rev['purchase_id'])
    # rating already float string
    rev['rating'] = float(rev['rating'])

# Filter books with year not None and rating_number > 0
books_with_year = [b for b in books if b['year'] is not None and int(b['rating_number'])>0]

# Create DataFrames
books_df = pd.DataFrame(books_with_year)
reviews_df = pd.DataFrame(reviews)

# Join on id_num
merged = reviews_df.merge(books_df, left_on='id_num', right_on='id_num', how='inner')

# Group by decade and compute stats
grouped = merged.groupby('decade').agg(
    distinct_books=('book_id', 'nunique'),
    avg_rating=('rating', 'mean')
).reset_index()

# Filter decades with at least 10 distinct books
filtered = grouped[grouped['distinct_books'] >= 10].copy()

# Sort by avg_rating descending
filtered_sorted = filtered.sort_values('avg_rating', ascending=False)

print('Decades with >=10 distinct books:')
print(filtered_sorted.head(10))

# Save to result
result = filtered_sorted.head(1).to_dict(orient='records')[0] if not filtered_sorted.empty else {}
print('Best decade:', result)

# Output
print("__RESULT__:")
print(json.dumps({
    "decade_data": filtered_sorted.to_dict(orient='records'),
    "best_decade": result
}))
"}

function.arguments:
{"code": "import json
import re
import pandas as pd

# Load data
books_file = locals().get('var_functions.query_db:28')
reviews_file = locals().get('var_functions.query_db:26')

with open(books_file, 'r') as f:
    books = json.load(f)
with open(reviews_file, 'r') as f:
    reviews = json.load(f)

# Process books: extract year and decade
books_processed = []
for book in books:
    # Extract numeric ID
    id_match = re.search(r'(\d+)', book['book_id'])
    id_num = id_match.group(1) if id_match else None
    
    # Extract year from details/subtitle (look for 4-digit years)
    text = f"{book.get('details', '')} {book.get('subtitle', '')}"
    years = re.findall(r'\b(19|20)\d{2}\b', text)
    year = int(years[0]) if years else None
    
    # Compute decade
    decade = f"{year//10*10}s" if year else None
    
    rating_number = int(book['rating_number'])
    
    books_processed.append({
        'book_id': book['book_id'],
        'id_num': id_num,
        'year': year,
        'decade': decade,
        'rating_number': rating_number
    })

# Process reviews
reviews_processed = []
for rev in reviews:
    id_match = re.search(r'(\d+)', rev['purchase_id'])
    id_num = id_match.group(1) if id_match else None
    rating = float(rev['rating'])
    
    reviews_processed.append({
        'purchase_id': rev['purchase_id'],
        'id_num': id_num,
        'rating': rating
    })

# Filter out None id_num
books_df = pd.DataFrame([b for b in books_processed if b['id_num'] is not None and b['year'] is not None and b['rating_number']>0])
reviews_df = pd.DataFrame([r for r in reviews_processed if r['id_num'] is not None])

print(f"Books with valid data: {len(books_df)}")
print(f"Reviews with valid data: {len(reviews_df)}")

# Merge on id_num
merged = pd.merge(reviews_df, books_df, on='id_num', how='inner')
print(f"Merged rows: {len(merged)}")
print("Sample merged:", merged.head())

# Group by decade
grouped = merged.groupby('decade').agg(
    distinct_books=('book_id', 'nunique'),
    avg_rating=('rating', 'mean')
).reset_index()

# Filter decades with at least 10 distinct books
filtered = grouped[grouped['distinct_books'] >= 10].copy()

# Sort by avg_rating
result_df = filtered.sort_values('avg_rating', ascending=False)

print("Decades with >=10 books:")
print(result_df)

# Get best decade
best = result_df.iloc[0].to_dict() if not result_df.empty else {}

# Output
final = {
    'decades': result_df.to_dict('records'),
    'best_decade': best
}

print("__RESULT__:")
print(json.dumps(final, indent=2))"}

{"code": "import json
import re

# The variables contain file paths
books_path = locals()['var_functions.query_db:28']
reviews_path = locals()['var_functions.query_db:26']

# Load data from files
with open(books_path, 'r') as f:
    books = json.load(f)
with open(reviews_path, 'r') as f:
    reviews = json.load(f)

print("Books loaded:", len(books))
print("Reviews loaded:", len(reviews))

# Extract years from book details/subtitles
books_with_decade = []
for book in books:
    try:
        rating_num = int(book['rating_number'])
        if rating_num == 0:
            continue
            
        # Extract year using regex on details and subtitle
        text = book.get('details', '') + ' ' + book.get('subtitle', '')
        years = re.findall(r'\b(19|20)\d{2}\b', text)
        
        if years:
            year = int(years[0])
            decade = f"{year//10*10}s"
            # Extract book number
            book_num_match = re.search(r'bookid_(\d+)', book['book_id'])
            if book_num_match:
                book_num = book_num_match.group(1)
                books_with_decade.append({
                    'book_id': book['book_id'],
                    'book_num': book_num,
                    'decade': decade,
                    'year': year
                })
    except:
        continue

print("Books with decade extracted:", len(books_with_decade))

# Process reviews - extract purchase numbers
reviews_processed = []
for review in reviews:
    try:
        purchase_num_match = re.search(r'purchaseid_(\d+)', review['purchase_id'])
        if purchase_num_match:
            purchase_num = purchase_num_match.group(1)
            rating = float(review['rating'])
            reviews_processed.append({
                'purchase_id': review['purchase_id'],
                'purchase_num': purchase_num,
                'rating': rating
            })
    except:
        continue

print("Reviews processed:", len(reviews_processed))

# Create dictionaries for lookup
books_dict = {b['book_num']: b for b in books_with_decade}

# Match reviews to books
matched = []
for rev in reviews_processed:
    if rev['purchase_num'] in books_dict:
        book = books_dict[rev['purchase_num']]
        matched.append({
            'book_id': book['book_id'],
            'decade': book['decade'],
            'rating': rev['rating']
        })

print("Matched reviews to books:", len(matched))

# Group by decade
from collections import defaultdict
decades = defaultdict(lambda: {'books': set(), 'ratings': [], 'count': 0})

for item in matched:
    decade = item['decade']
    decades[decade]['books'].add(item['book_id'])
    decades[decade]['ratings'].append(item['rating'])
    decades[decade]['count'] += 1

# Filter decades with at least 10 distinct books
filtered = []
for decade, data in decades.items():
    distinct_books = len(data['books'])
    if distinct_books >= 10:
        avg_rating = sum(data['ratings']) / len(data['ratings'])
        filtered.append({
            'decade': decade,
            'distinct_books': distinct_books,
            'avg_rating': avg_rating,
            'total_ratings': data['count']
        })

# Sort by avg rating
filtered.sort(key=lambda x: x['avg_rating'], reverse=True)

print("Decades with >=10 books:", len(filtered))
for d in filtered[:5]:
    print(d)

# Get best decade
best = filtered[0] if filtered else None

result = {
    'best_decade': best,
    'all_decades': filtered
}

print("__RESULT__:")
print(json.dumps(result, indent=2))"}

{"code": "import json
import re
from collections import defaultdict

# Load data
books_path = locals()['var_functions.query_db:28']
reviews_path = locals()['var_functions.query_db:26']

with open(books_path, 'r') as f:
    books = json.load(f)
with open(reviews_path, 'r') as f:
    reviews = json.load(f)

# Extract book numbers and publication years
books_dict = {}
for book in books:
    # Extract book number
    book_num_match = re.search(r'bookid_(\d+)', book['book_id'])
    if not book_num_match:
        continue
    book_num = book_num_match.group(1)
    
    # Extract year from details/subtitle
    text = book.get('details', '') + ' ' + book.get('subtitle', '')
    years = re.findall(r'\b(19|20)\d{2}\b', text)
    
    if years:
        year = int(years[0])
        decade = f"{year//10*10}s"
        books_dict[book_num] = {
            'book_id': book['book_id'],
            'year': year,
            'decade': decade
        }

# Process reviews
reviews_by_book = defaultdict(list)
for review in reviews:
    # Extract the book number from purchase_id
    purchase_match = re.search(r'purchaseid_(\d+)', review['purchase_id'])
    if not purchase_match:
        continue
    book_num = purchase_match.group(1)
    
    # Extract rating
    try:
        rating = float(review['rating'])
        reviews_by_book[book_num].append(rating)
    except:
        continue

# Group by decade
decade_data = defaultdict(lambda: {'books': set(), 'ratings': []})

for book_num, book_info in books_dict.items():
    if book_num in reviews_by_book:
        decade = book_info['decade']
        decade_data[decade]['books'].add(book_info['book_id'])
        decade_data[decade]['ratings'].extend(reviews_by_book[book_num])

# Filter decades with at least 10 books and compute average ratings
results = []
for decade, data in decade_data.items():
    distinct_books = len(data['books'])
    if distinct_books >= 10:
        avg_rating = sum(data['ratings']) / len(data['ratings'])
        results.append({
            'decade': decade,
            'distinct_books': distinct_books,
            'avg_rating': avg_rating,
            'total_reviews': len(data['ratings'])
        })

# Sort by average rating descending
results.sort(key=lambda x: x['avg_rating'], reverse=True)

print('Found', len(results), 'decades with >=10 distinct books')
for r in results[:5]:
    print(r)

# Create final result
final_result = {
    'best_decade': results[0] if results else None,
    'all_decades': results
}

print("__RESULT__:")
print(json.dumps(final_result, indent=2))"}

{"code": "import json
import re
from collections import defaultdict

# Load data
books_path = locals()['var_functions.query_db:28']
reviews_path = locals()['var_functions.query_db:26']

with open(books_path, 'r') as f:
    books = json.load(f)
with open(reviews_path, 'r') as f:
    reviews = json.load(f)

# Show ID patterns
print("Sample book IDs (first 20):")
for book in books[:20]:
    print(f"  {book['book_id']}")

print("\nSample review purchase IDs (first 20):")
for review in reviews[:20]:
    print(f"  {review['purchase_id']}")

# Check if purchase_id matches book_id directly
print("\nMatching directly...")
book_ids_set = set(book['book_id'] for book in books)
review_ids_set = set(review['purchase_id'] for review in reviews)

print(f"Unique book IDs: {len(book_ids_set)}")
print(f"Unique review purchase IDs: {len(review_ids_set)}")

# Count exact matches
exact_matches = book_ids_set.intersection(review_ids_set)
print(f"Direct ID matches: {len(exact_matches)}")

if not exact_matches:
    # Try to extract numeric IDs and match those
    print("\nNo direct matches, trying numeric extraction...")
    
    # Extract book numbers
    book_nums = {}
    for book in books:
        match = re.search(r'bookid_(\d+)', book['book_id'])
        if match:
            book_nums[match.group(1)] = book
    
    # Extract review numbers  
    review_nums = {}
    for review in reviews:
        # Try different patterns
        match1 = re.search(r'purchaseid_(\d+)', review['purchase_id'])
        if match1:
            review_nums[match1.group(1)] = review
        else:
            match2 = re.search(r'purchaseid_(\d+)', review['purchase_id'])
            if match2:
                review_nums[match2.group(1)] = review
    
    print(f"Books with extractable numbers: {len(book_nums)}")
    print(f"Reviews with extractable numbers: {len(review_nums)}")
    
    # Count overlapping numbers
    common_nums = set(book_nums.keys()).intersection(review_nums.keys())
    print(f"Overlapping numeric IDs: {len(common_nums)}")
    
    if common_nums:
        print("Sample matches:")
        sample = list(common_nums)[:5]
        for num in sample:
            print(f"  Book ID: {book_nums[num]['book_id']}")
            print(f"  Review ID: {review_nums[num]['purchase_id']}")

print("\nDone")"}

{"code": "import json
import re
from collections import defaultdict
import statistics

# Load data
with open(locals()['var_functions.query_db:28'], 'r') as f:
    books = json.load(f)
with open(locals()['var_functions.query_db:26'], 'r') as f:
    reviews = json.load(f)

# Build dictionaries by numeric ID
books_dict = {}
for book in books:
    # Extract numeric ID
    num_match = re.search(r'bookid_(\d+)', book['book_id'])
    if num_match:
        num = num_match.group(1)
        # Extract publication year
        text = book.get('details', '') + ' ' + book.get('subtitle', '')
        years = re.findall(r'\b(19|20)\d{2}\b', text)
        year = int(years[0]) if years else None
        decade = f"{year//10*10}s" if year else None
        
        books_dict[num] = {
            'book_id': book['book_id'],
            'year': year,
            'decade': decade
        }

# Group reviews by book number
reviews_by_book = defaultdict(list)
for review in reviews:
    num_match = re.search(r'purchaseid_(\d+)', review['purchase_id'])
    if num_match:
        num = num_match.group(1)
        rating = float(review['rating'])
        reviews_by_book[num].append(rating)

# Calculate average rating per book
book_ratings = {}
for num, ratings in reviews_by_book.items():
    if num in books_dict:
        avg_rating = statistics.mean(ratings)
        book_ratings[num] = {
            'decade': books_dict[num]['decade'],
            'avg_rating': avg_rating,
            'review_count': len(ratings),
            'book_id': books_dict[num]['book_id']
        }

print(f"Books with reviews: {len(book_ratings)}")

# Group by decade
from collections import defaultdict

decade_stats = defaultdict(lambda: {'books': set(), 'ratings': [], 'review_counts': []})

for num, data in book_ratings.items():
    decade = data['decade']
    if decade:
        decade_stats[decade]['books'].add(data['book_id'])
        decade_stats[decade]['ratings'].append(data['avg_rating'])
        decade_stats[decade]['review_counts'].append(data['review_count'])

print(f"Decades found: {len(decade_stats)}")

# Filter decades with at least 10 distinct books and compute averages
results = []
for decade, data in decade_stats.items():
    distinct_books = len(data['books'])
    if distinct_books >= 10:
        avg_rating = statistics.mean(data['ratings'])
        total_reviews = sum(data['review_counts'])
        results.append({
            'decade': decade,
            'distinct_books': distinct_books,
            'avg_rating': avg_rating,
            'total_reviews': total_reviews
        })

# Sort by average rating
results.sort(key=lambda x: x['avg_rating'], reverse=True)

print(f"Decades with >=10 books: {len(results)}")
for r in results:
    print(f"{r['decade']}: {r['distinct_books']} books, avg={r['avg_rating']:.3f}, reviews={r['total_reviews']}")

# Get best decade
best = results[0] if results else None

result = {
    'best_decade': best,
    'all_decades': results
}

print("__RESULT__:")
print(json.dumps(result, indent=2))"}

{"code": "import json
import re
from collections import defaultdict

# Load data again
books_path = locals()['var_functions.query_db:28']
reviews_path = locals()['var_functions.query_db:26']

with open(books_path, 'r') as f:
    books = json.load(f)
with open(reviews_path, 'r') as f:
    reviews = json.load(f)

# Build book data with decades
books_with_decades = {}
for book in books:
    # Extract numeric ID
    num_match = re.search(r'bookid_(\d+)', book['book_id'])
    if not num_match:
        continue
    book_num = num_match.group(1)
    
    # Extract year
    text = f"{book.get('details', '')} {book.get('subtitle', '')}"
    years = re.findall(r'\b(19|20)\d{2}\b', text)
    if not years:
        continue
    
    year = int(years[0])
    decade = f"{year//10*10}s"
    
    books_with_decades[book_num] = {
        'book_id': book['book_id'],
        'year': year,
        'decade': decade
    }

# Build reviews by book number
reviews_by_booknum = defaultdict(list)
for review in reviews:
    num_match = re.search(r'purchaseid_(\d+)', review['purchase_id'])
    if not num_match:
        continue
    book_num = num_match.group(1)
    rating = float(review['rating'])
    reviews_by_booknum[book_num].append(rating)

# Check actual matches
print("Books with decades:", len(books_with_decades))
print("Books with reviews:", len(reviews_by_booknum))

# Find common book numbers
common_books = set(books_with_decades.keys()).intersection(reviews_by_booknum.keys())
print("Books with both decade and reviews:", len(common_books))

# Sample a few matches
sample_common = list(common_books)[:5]
print("\nSample matches:")
for num in sample_common:
    book = books_with_decades[num]
    reviews = reviews_by_booknum[num]
    print(f"Book {book['book_id']}: {book['decade']}, {len(reviews)} reviews")

# Now calculate properly
decade_ratings = defaultdict(lambda: defaultdict(list))

for book_num in common_books:
    book = books_with_decades[book_num]
    ratings = reviews_by_booknum[book_num]
    decade = book['decade']
    
    decade_ratings[decade]['ratings'].extend(ratings)
    decade_ratings[decade]['books'].add(book['book_id'])

# Calculate stats
results = []
for decade, data in decade_ratings.items():
    distinct_books = len(data['books'])
    if distinct_books >= 10:
        avg_rating = sum(data['ratings']) / len(data['ratings'])
        results.append({
            'decade': decade,
            'distinct_books': distinct_books,
            'avg_rating': avg_rating,
            'total_reviews': len(data['ratings'])
        })

# Sort
results.sort(key=lambda x: x['avg_rating'], reverse=True)

print("\nDecades with >=10 books:", len(results))
for r in results:
    print(f"{r['decade']}: {r['distinct_books']} books, avg={r['avg_rating']:.3f}")

# Get best decade
best = results[0] if results else None

final = {
    'best_decade': best,
    'total_decades': len(results)
}

print("__RESULT__:")
print(json.dumps(final, indent=2))"}

from collections import defaultdict

with open(books_path, 'r') as f:
    books = json.load(f)
with open(reviews_path, 'r') as f:
    reviews = json.load(f)

books_with_decades = {}
for book in books:
    m = re.search(r'bookid_(\d+)', book['book_id'])
    if not m:
        continue
    num = m.group(1)
    
    text = book.get('details', '') + ' ' + book.get('subtitle', '')
    years = re.findall(r'\b(19|20)\d{2}\b', text)
    if not years:
        continue
    
    year = int(years[0])
    decade = f"{year//10*10}s"
    
    books_with_decades[num] = {
        'book_id': book['book_id'],
        'decade': decade
    }

reviews_by_num = defaultdict(list)
for review in reviews:
    m = re.search(r'purchaseid_(\d+)', review['purchase_id'])
    if m:
        num = m.group(1)
        rating = float(review['rating'])
        reviews_by_num[num].append(rating)

common = set(books_with_decades.keys()).intersection(reviews_by_num.keys())
decade_group = defaultdict(lambda: {'books': set(), 'ratings': []})

for num in common:
    decade = books_with_decades[num]['decade']
    decade_group[decade]['books'].add(books_with_decades[num]['book_id'])
    decade_group[decade]['ratings'].extend(reviews_by_num[num])

results = []
for decade, data in decade_group.items():
    distinct_books = len(data['books'])
    if distinct_books >= 10:
        avg_rating = sum(data['ratings']) / len(data['ratings'])

            'decade': decade,
            'distinct_books': distinct_books,
            'avg_rating': avg_rating
        })

results.sort(key=lambda x: x['avg_rating'], reverse=True)

best = results[0] if results else None
final = {'best_decade': best, 'total_decades': len(results)}

function.arguments:
{"code": "import json
import re
from collections import defaultdict

# Load data
books_path = locals()['var_functions.query_db:28']
reviews_path = locals()['var_functions.query_db:26']
with open(books_path, 'r') as f:
    books = json.load(f)
with open(reviews_path, 'r') as f:
    reviews = json.load(f)

# Build books dictionary with decade
books_dict = {}
for book in books:
    # Get numeric ID
    m = re.search(r'bookid_(\d+)', book['book_id'])
    if not m:
        continue
    book_num = m.group(1)
    
    # Extract year from details or subtitle
    text = (book.get('details', '') or '') + ' ' + (book.get('subtitle', '') or '')
    years = re.findall(r'\b(19|20)\d{2}\b', text)
    if not years:
        continue
    
    year = int(years[0])
    decade = str(year // 10 * 10) + 's'
    
    books_dict[book_num] = {
        'book_id': book['book_id'],
        'decade': decade,
        'year': year
    }

# Build reviews dictionary
reviews_dict = {}
for review in reviews:
    m = re.search(r'purchaseid_(\d+)', review['purchase_id'])
    if m:
        book_num = m.group(1)
        rating = float(review['rating'])
        if book_num not in reviews_dict:
            reviews_dict[book_num] = []
        reviews_dict[book_num].append(rating)

print(f"Books with decade: {len(books_dict)}")
print(f"Books with reviews: {len(reviews_dict)}")

# Count reviews per decade
decade_counts = defaultdict(lambda: {'books': set(), 'ratings': []})

for book_num, data in books_dict.items():
    if book_num in reviews_dict:
        decade = data['decade']
        decade_counts[decade]['books'].add(data['book_id'])
        decade_counts[decade]['ratings'].extend(reviews_dict[book_num])

print(f"Decades found: {len(decade_counts)}")

# Filter and calculate results
results = []
for decade, data in decade_counts.items():
    distinct_books = len(data['books'])
    if distinct_books >= 10:
        avg_rating = sum(data['ratings']) / len(data['ratings'])
        results.append({
            'decade': decade,
            'distinct_books': distinct_books,
            'avg_rating': avg_rating,
            'total_ratings': len(data['ratings'])
        })

results.sort(key=lambda x: x['avg_rating'], reverse=True)

best = results[0] if results else None
output = {'best_decade': best, 'count': len(results)}

print('__RESULT__:')
print(json.dumps(output))"}

- list_db: list tables or collections for a given database.
  Required args: {"db_name": "<logical_db_name>"}
- execute_python: run Python code.
  Required args: {"code": "<python_code>"}
- return_answer: finish and return the final answer (plain text).
  Required args: {"answer": "<final plain-text answer>"}

INSTRUCTIONS:
1. After each tool call, its result will be stored in a storage under a key named after the tool call id (you will be told the key name). The next message will include both the result (or a preview if it's large) and the storage key name.
2. Inside execute_python code you may read storage entries using the provided key names, e.g., if the tool call id is 'functions.tool:id', you can access its result via `locals()['var_functions.tool:id']` in your code.
3. You cannot modify or reassign those storage-provided variables; you may read them and create new variables as needed.
4. If a tool result is large, the next message will include a preview (first 10000 characters) and the storage entry will be the .json file path (a string) where the full result is stored. To access the full result, your execute_python code must open and read that .json file.

2. Include all required arguments for the tool you call.

4. For PostgreSQL, wrap mixed-case or uppercase column names in double quotes.
5. For list_db, refer the DATABASE DESCRIPTION to specify db_name.
6. Use execute_python for data processing as needed. 
7. When using execute_python, your code will be quoted by triple double-quotes and passed as a string to `exec(...)` for execution in a Python 3.12 environment with only pandas and pyarrow installed. So you must ensure your code is compatible with this execution method. For exampe, do not use triple double-quotes in your code, as they may interfere with parsing. Do not use non-built-in or non-installed packages.

PRINT FORMAT (must match exactly):
----BEGIN PRINT FORMAT----

----END PRINT FORMAT----

For simple types (int, float, str, bool, None), you may use json.dumps() to produce a valid JSON string.
For complex or non-JSON-serializable types, you must convert them into JSON-compatible forms before printing.
For lists or dictionaries, you must ensure that all nested elements are also converted into JSON-serializable types.
9. Return the final answer only via a single return_answer tool call. Do not include extra text, explanation, or formatting.

print('__RESULT__:')
print(result)"}}

If you cannot proceed, call return_answer with a short explanatory message.

Do not output explanations, reasoning, or any natural language outside of the required tool calls.

QUERY:
Which decade of publication (e.g., 1980s) has the highest average rating among decades with at least 10 distinct books that have been rated? Return the decade with the highest average rating.

DATABASE DESCRIPTION:
You are working with two databases to solve this query.

Here are the descriptions of these two databases:

1. review_database
   - This database is stored in a SQLite database and contains Amazon book review information including ratings, text, helpfulness votes, etc. up to 2023.
   - This database consists of one table:
    - review_database:
      - Fields:
        - rating (float): Rating given by reviewer (1.0-5.0 scale)
        - title (str): Book title
        - text (str): Review text content
        - purchase_id (str): Unique identifier linking to book_id in books_database
        - review_time (str): Timestamp when review was posted
        - helpful_vote (int): Number of helpful votes received
        - verified_purchase (bool): Whether purchase was verified

2. books_database
   - This database is stored in a PostgreSQL database and contains Amazon book information including descriptions, price, details, title, etc. up to 2023.
   - This database consists of one table:
    - books_info:
      - Fields:
        - title (str): Book title
        - subtitle (str): Book subtitle
        - author (str): Book author(s)
        - rating_number (int): Total number of ratings received
        - features (str): Book features (stored as string representation of list/dict)
        - description (str): Book description (stored as string representation of list/dict)
        - price (float): Book price
        - store (str): Store information
        - categories (str): Book categories (stored as string representation of list/dict)
        - details (str): Additional book details
        - book_id (str): Unique book identifier

HINTS: 
- In books_info, the "description", "categories", and "features" content appears to be in list or dictionary format, but they are actually stored as strings in the .sql file.
- The fields "book_id" in books_info and "purchase_id" in review_query.db refer to the same book entities across different tables. While the field names are not exact matches, they can be joined using a fuzzy join approach to solve the query.
- For some queries, you could get needed information from "categories" or "details" columns in books_database.

The tool query_db was executed successfully. The result is stored under key: var_functions.query_db:16 The result is: [{"rating": "4", "title": "Ha! On me! I thought this was a cookbook!", "text": "Lucky for me it's more than a cookbook.

The story of a modern Daniel Boone, growing up in Michigan, one of three sons of a hunter. Beginning at age eight when he shot a squirrel, to age 13 his first deer, then hunting his way through college, a career as a fur trapper, hunting sheep in Alaska, canoeing the Missouri River for deer and ending in Brooklyn age 37. Brooklyn?

I especially liked that I share his disgust at catch & release fishing - if you fish it, you have to eat it - anything else is sadism (my words).

My brother in law is the cook in the family and I wanted to see if this would be a good gift for him and I struck pay dirt following each chapter are \"Tasting Notes\" where the author speaks of cooking wild game.

I also enjoyed it for the history of the land he has hunted as well as the history of the hunt (I too was a big Daniel Boone fan growing up!).

I think more pictures (everything but dead things) would have made this a keeper.", "review_time": "2012-11-24 18:52:00", "helpful_vote": "0", "verified_purchase": "0", "purchase_id": "purchaseid_186"}, {"rating": "4", "title": "Four Stars", "text": "Not as developed as Stephanie but I like the characters so far.", "review_time": "2015-12-31 13:35:00", "helpful_vote": "0", "verified_purchase": "1", "purchase_id": "purchaseid_191"}, {"rating": "4", "title": "A wonderful adventure in France", "text": "I loved this book all the way until the end. I have recently discovered that the author is intending to release another book, and from what I understand it will continue where this one left off. I am thankful for this, as the way this book ended was definitely a negative for me. I wanted to know more! What happened to the author, did she learn what she came to learn? Did she get the job she was hoping for? So many questions.

From the beginning of Linda's book, it is easy to love her. She is open, honest and definitely has the type of personality you want your heroine to have, whether the book is fiction or reality. I couldn't help but root for Linda throughout her trials with her host family, even when I thought she acted as a bit of a brat herself. Listening to her internal thoughts about what she went through and her desire to achieve her goals made this book feel close to my heart.

I applaud the adventurous spirit of the author and her decision to keep journal entries from that chapter of her life. What great material to have later to inspire a book! While I enjoyed the entire memoir, my favourite part of this book would have to be the author's descriptions of the many places she visited and the people she met along the way. While I think she could definitely have made a better impression on the family she worked for if she had been honest about her lack of French language skills from the beginning, she is a pioneer. Her drive and desire to learn the language from those in the actual country was inspiring. Not many people would have been gutsy enough to do what Linda did.

I also particularly enjoyed the relationship between Linda and Antoine. The Kind heart of the author was apparent in her actions toward the children, even when she struggled with them.

There is some romance, and I appreciated the way the author handled it. While sex scenes are not necessarily automatically offensive, there is something about memoirs that makes me uncomfortable if they are filled with them. This author manages to hint at her experiences without going into too much detail, leaving the reader to decide exactly what happened. Great writing.

When you step into reading this book, you learn a lot about French customs, French cuisine and wine. You also get the opportunity to brush up on your French skills a bit and get to step outside yourself and live an adventure through the eyes of the author. I read this book in one sitting with very few breaks in between and found myself enthralled in the sights, sounds and atmosphere of this memoir.

In the end, I was disappointed that the book ended. I could have kept reading about Linda's experiences without stopping anytime in the foreseeable future. The end of the book leaves many unanswered questions, that I am assuming the author will be answering in her next book. Were this a standalone, I would be very disappointed with the way the book ended, but with the knowledge that she is writing another book to continue with her story, I can't fault this one. One thing is for sure, if you read this book, you will be anxiously awaiting the next.

I would recommend this to anyone who likes books about travel, memoirs or just a good story that came from the recollections and heart of the author. Read it, you will be glad you did. Overall, this was a VERY enjoyable read that gave me many reasons to wish I could drop everything and head off to France!", "review_time": "2013-05-05 10:47:00", "helpful_vote": "1", "verified_purchase": "0", "purchase_id": "purchaseid_190"}]

{"code": "import json
import re
import pandas as pd

# Load data
books_file = locals().get('var_functions.query_db:28')
reviews_file = locals().get('var_functions.query_db:26')

with open(books_file, 'r') as f:
    books = json.load(f)
with open(reviews_file, 'r') as f:
    reviews = json.load(f)

# Helper to extract numeric ID
extract_number = lambda s: re.search(r'(\d+)', s).group(1) if re.search(r'(\d+)', s) else None

# Process books: add numeric id and year
for book in books:
    book['id_num'] = extract_number(book['book_id'])
    # Extract year from details and subtitle
    text = f"{book.get('details', '')} {book.get('subtitle', '')}"
    # Find 4-digit years between 1900-2023
    years = re.findall(r'(\b(19|20)\d{2}\b)', text)
    year = None
    if years:
        year = int(years[0][0])
    book['year'] = year
    # Compute decade
    if year:
        decade = f"{year//10*10}s"
    else:
        decade = None
    book['decade'] = decade

# Process reviews: add numeric id
for rev in reviews:
    rev['id_num'] = extract_number(rev['purchase_id'])
    # rating already float string
    rev['rating'] = float(rev['rating'])

# Filter books with year not None and rating_number > 0
books_with_year = [b for b in books if b['year'] is not None and int(b['rating_number'])>0]

# Create DataFrames
books_df = pd.DataFrame(books_with_year)
reviews_df = pd.DataFrame(reviews)

# Join on id_num
merged = reviews_df.merge(books_df, left_on='id_num', right_on='id_num', how='inner')

# Group by decade and compute stats
grouped = merged.groupby('decade').agg(
    distinct_books=('book_id', 'nunique'),
    avg_rating=('rating', 'mean')
).reset_index()

# Filter decades with at least 10 distinct books
filtered = grouped[grouped['distinct_books'] >= 10].copy()

# Sort by avg_rating descending
filtered_sorted = filtered.sort_values('avg_rating', ascending=False)

print('Decades with >=10 distinct books:')
print(filtered_sorted.head(10))

# Save to result
result = filtered_sorted.head(1).to_dict(orient='records')[0] if not filtered_sorted.empty else {}
print('Best decade:', result)

# Output
print("__RESULT__:")
print(json.dumps({
    "decade_data": filtered_sorted.to_dict(orient='records'),
    "best_decade": result
}))
"}

books_processed = []
for book in books:
    # Extract numeric ID
    id_match = re.search(r'(\d+)', book['book_id'])
    id_num = id_match.group(1) if id_match else None
    
    # Extract year from details/subtitle (look for 4-digit years)
    text = f"{book.get('details', '')} {book.get('subtitle', '')}"
    years = re.findall(r'\b(19|20)\d{2}\b', text)

    # Compute decade

    })

# Process reviews

for rev in reviews:

print(f"Reviews with valid data: {len(reviews_df)}")

{"code": "import json
import re

# The variables contain file paths
books_path = locals()['var_functions.query_db:28']
reviews_path = locals()['var_functions.query_db:26']

# Load data from files
with open(books_path, 'r') as f:
    books = json.load(f)
with open(reviews_path, 'r') as f:
    reviews = json.load(f)

print("Books loaded:", len(books))
print("Reviews loaded:", len(reviews))

# Extract years from book details/subtitles
books_with_decade = []
for book in books:
    try:
        rating_num = int(book['rating_number'])
        if rating_num == 0:
            continue
            
        # Extract year using regex on details and subtitle
        text = book.get('details', '') + ' ' + book.get('subtitle', '')
        years = re.findall(r'\b(19|20)\d{2}\b', text)
        
        if years:
            year = int(years[0])
            decade = f"{year//10*10}s"
            # Extract book number
            book_num_match = re.search(r'bookid_(\d+)', book['book_id'])
            if book_num_match:
                book_num = book_num_match.group(1)
                books_with_decade.append({
                    'book_id': book['book_id'],
                    'book_num': book_num,
                    'decade': decade,
                    'year': year
                })
    except:
        continue

print("Books with decade extracted:", len(books_with_decade))

# Process reviews - extract purchase numbers
reviews_processed = []
for review in reviews:
    try:
        purchase_num_match = re.search(r'purchaseid_(\d+)', review['purchase_id'])
        if purchase_num_match:
            purchase_num = purchase_num_match.group(1)
            rating = float(review['rating'])
            reviews_processed.append({
                'purchase_id': review['purchase_id'],
                'purchase_num': purchase_num,
                'rating': rating
            })
    except:
        continue

print("Reviews processed:", len(reviews_processed))

# Create dictionaries for lookup
books_dict = {b['book_num']: b for b in books_with_decade}

# Match reviews to books
matched = []
for rev in reviews_processed:
    if rev['purchase_num'] in books_dict:
        book = books_dict[rev['purchase_num']]
        matched.append({
            'book_id': book['book_id'],
            'decade': book['decade'],
            'rating': rev['rating']
        })

print("Matched reviews to books:", len(matched))

# Group by decade
from collections import defaultdict
decades = defaultdict(lambda: {'books': set(), 'ratings': [], 'count': 0})

for item in matched:
    decade = item['decade']
    decades[decade]['books'].add(item['book_id'])
    decades[decade]['ratings'].append(item['rating'])
    decades[decade]['count'] += 1

# Filter decades with at least 10 distinct books
filtered = []
for decade, data in decades.items():
    distinct_books = len(data['books'])
    if distinct_books >= 10:
        avg_rating = sum(data['ratings']) / len(data['ratings'])
        filtered.append({
            'decade': decade,
            'distinct_books': distinct_books,
            'avg_rating': avg_rating,
            'total_ratings': data['count']
        })

# Sort by avg rating
filtered.sort(key=lambda x: x['avg_rating'], reverse=True)

print("Decades with >=10 books:", len(filtered))
for d in filtered[:5]:
    print(d)

# Get best decade
best = filtered[0] if filtered else None

result = {
    'best_decade': best,
    'all_decades': filtered
}

print("__RESULT__:")
print(json.dumps(result, indent=2))"}

{"code": "import json
import re
from collections import defaultdict

# Load data
books_path = locals()['var_functions.query_db:28']
reviews_path = locals()['var_functions.query_db:26']

with open(books_path, 'r') as f:
    books = json.load(f)
with open(reviews_path, 'r') as f:
    reviews = json.load(f)

# Extract book numbers and publication years
books_dict = {}
for book in books:
    # Extract book number
    book_num_match = re.search(r'bookid_(\d+)', book['book_id'])
    if not book_num_match:
        continue
    book_num = book_num_match.group(1)
    
    # Extract year from details/subtitle
    text = book.get('details', '') + ' ' + book.get('subtitle', '')
    years = re.findall(r'\b(19|20)\d{2}\b', text)
    
    if years:
        year = int(years[0])
        decade = f"{year//10*10}s"
        books_dict[book_num] = {
            'book_id': book['book_id'],
            'year': year,
            'decade': decade
        }

# Process reviews
reviews_by_book = defaultdict(list)
for review in reviews:
    # Extract the book number from purchase_id
    purchase_match = re.search(r'purchaseid_(\d+)', review['purchase_id'])
    if not purchase_match:
        continue
    book_num = purchase_match.group(1)
    
    # Extract rating
    try:
        rating = float(review['rating'])
        reviews_by_book[book_num].append(rating)
    except:
        continue

# Group by decade
decade_data = defaultdict(lambda: {'books': set(), 'ratings': []})

for book_num, book_info in books_dict.items():
    if book_num in reviews_by_book:
        decade = book_info['decade']
        decade_data[decade]['books'].add(book_info['book_id'])
        decade_data[decade]['ratings'].extend(reviews_by_book[book_num])

# Filter decades with at least 10 books and compute average ratings
results = []
for decade, data in decade_data.items():
    distinct_books = len(data['books'])
    if distinct_books >= 10:
        avg_rating = sum(data['ratings']) / len(data['ratings'])
        results.append({
            'decade': decade,
            'distinct_books': distinct_books,
            'avg_rating': avg_rating,
            'total_reviews': len(data['ratings'])
        })

# Sort by average rating descending
results.sort(key=lambda x: x['avg_rating'], reverse=True)

print('Found', len(results), 'decades with >=10 distinct books')
for r in results[:5]:
    print(r)

# Create final result
final_result = {
    'best_decade': results[0] if results else None,
    'all_decades': results
}

print("__RESULT__:")
print(json.dumps(final_result, indent=2))"}

{"code": "import json
import re
from collections import defaultdict

# Load data
books_path = locals()['var_functions.query_db:28']
reviews_path = locals()['var_functions.query_db:26']

with open(books_path, 'r') as f:
    books = json.load(f)
with open(reviews_path, 'r') as f:
    reviews = json.load(f)

# Show ID patterns
print("Sample book IDs (first 20):")
for book in books[:20]:
    print(f"  {book['book_id']}")

print("\nSample review purchase IDs (first 20):")
for review in reviews[:20]:
    print(f"  {review['purchase_id']}")

# Check if purchase_id matches book_id directly
print("\nMatching directly...")
book_ids_set = set(book['book_id'] for book in books)
review_ids_set = set(review['purchase_id'] for review in reviews)

print(f"Unique book IDs: {len(book_ids_set)}")
print(f"Unique review purchase IDs: {len(review_ids_set)}")

# Count exact matches
exact_matches = book_ids_set.intersection(review_ids_set)
print(f"Direct ID matches: {len(exact_matches)}")

if not exact_matches:
    # Try to extract numeric IDs and match those
    print("\nNo direct matches, trying numeric extraction...")
    
    # Extract book numbers
    book_nums = {}
    for book in books:
        match = re.search(r'bookid_(\d+)', book['book_id'])
        if match:
            book_nums[match.group(1)] = book
    
    # Extract review numbers  
    review_nums = {}
    for review in reviews:
        # Try different patterns
        match1 = re.search(r'purchaseid_(\d+)', review['purchase_id'])
        if match1:
            review_nums[match1.group(1)] = review
        else:
            match2 = re.search(r'purchaseid_(\d+)', review['purchase_id'])
            if match2:
                review_nums[match2.group(1)] = review
    
    print(f"Books with extractable numbers: {len(book_nums)}")
    print(f"Reviews with extractable numbers: {len(review_nums)}")
    
    # Count overlapping numbers
    common_nums = set(book_nums.keys()).intersection(review_nums.keys())
    print(f"Overlapping numeric IDs: {len(common_nums)}")
    
    if common_nums:
        print("Sample matches:")
        sample = list(common_nums)[:5]
        for num in sample:
            print(f"  Book ID: {book_nums[num]['book_id']}")
            print(f"  Review ID: {review_nums[num]['purchase_id']}")

print("\nDone")"}

{"code": "import json
import re
from collections import defaultdict
import statistics

# Load data
with open(locals()['var_functions.query_db:28'], 'r') as f:
    books = json.load(f)
with open(locals()['var_functions.query_db:26'], 'r') as f:
    reviews = json.load(f)

# Build dictionaries by numeric ID
books_dict = {}
for book in books:
    # Extract numeric ID
    num_match = re.search(r'bookid_(\d+)', book['book_id'])
    if num_match:
        num = num_match.group(1)
        # Extract publication year
        text = book.get('details', '') + ' ' + book.get('subtitle', '')
        years = re.findall(r'\b(19|20)\d{2}\b', text)
        year = int(years[0]) if years else None
        decade = f"{year//10*10}s" if year else None
        
        books_dict[num] = {
            'book_id': book['book_id'],
            'year': year,
            'decade': decade
        }

# Group reviews by book number
reviews_by_book = defaultdict(list)
for review in reviews:
    num_match = re.search(r'purchaseid_(\d+)', review['purchase_id'])
    if num_match:
        num = num_match.group(1)
        rating = float(review['rating'])
        reviews_by_book[num].append(rating)

# Calculate average rating per book
book_ratings = {}
for num, ratings in reviews_by_book.items():
    if num in books_dict:
        avg_rating = statistics.mean(ratings)
        book_ratings[num] = {
            'decade': books_dict[num]['decade'],
            'avg_rating': avg_rating,
            'review_count': len(ratings),
            'book_id': books_dict[num]['book_id']
        }

print(f"Books with reviews: {len(book_ratings)}")

# Group by decade
from collections import defaultdict

decade_stats = defaultdict(lambda: {'books': set(), 'ratings': [], 'review_counts': []})

for num, data in book_ratings.items():
    decade = data['decade']
    if decade:
        decade_stats[decade]['books'].add(data['book_id'])
        decade_stats[decade]['ratings'].append(data['avg_rating'])
        decade_stats[decade]['review_counts'].append(data['review_count'])

print(f"Decades found: {len(decade_stats)}")

# Filter decades with at least 10 distinct books and compute averages
results = []
for decade, data in decade_stats.items():
    distinct_books = len(data['books'])
    if distinct_books >= 10:
        avg_rating = statistics.mean(data['ratings'])
        total_reviews = sum(data['review_counts'])
        results.append({
            'decade': decade,
            'distinct_books': distinct_books,
            'avg_rating': avg_rating,
            'total_reviews': total_reviews
        })

# Sort by average rating
results.sort(key=lambda x: x['avg_rating'], reverse=True)

print(f"Decades with >=10 books: {len(results)}")
for r in results:
    print(f"{r['decade']}: {r['distinct_books']} books, avg={r['avg_rating']:.3f}, reviews={r['total_reviews']}")

# Get best decade
best = results[0] if results else None

result = {
    'best_decade': best,
    'all_decades': results
}

print("__RESULT__:")
print(json.dumps(result, indent=2))"}

{"code": "import json
import re
from collections import defaultdict

# Load data
books_path = locals()['var_functions.query_db:28']
reviews_path = locals()['var_functions.query_db:26']
with open(books_path, 'r') as f:
    books = json.load(f)
with open(reviews_path, 'r') as f:
    reviews = json.load(f)

# Extract years from books
books_with_years = []
for book in books:
    text = (book.get('details', '') or '') + ' ' + (book.get('subtitle', '') or '')
    years = re.findall(r'\b(19|20)\d{2}\b', text)
    if years:
        books_with_years.append({
            'book_id': book['book_id'],
            'year': int(years[0])
        })

print(f"Books with extractable years: {len(books_with_years)}")
print(f"Sample years: {[b['year'] for b in books_with_years[:10]]}")

# Count reviews per book number
reviews_per_book = defaultdict(int)
for review in reviews:
    m = re.search(r'purchaseid_(\d+)', review['purchase_id'])
    if m:
        book_num = m.group(1)
        reviews_per_book[book_num] += 1

print(f"Books with reviews: {len(reviews_per_book)}")
print(f"Review counts range: {min(reviews_per_book.values())} to {max(reviews_per_book.values())}")

# Show review distribution
from collections import Counter
review_counts_dist = Counter(reviews_per_book.values())
print("Review count distribution:")
for count, freq in sorted(review_counts_dist.items())[:10]:
    print(f"  {count} reviews: {freq} books")

# Map books to decades
decade_book_counts = defaultdict(int)
for book in books_with_years:
    m = re.search(r'bookid_(\d+)', book['book_id'])
    if m:
        book_num = m.group(1)
        if book_num in reviews_per_book:
            decade = f"{book['year']//10*10}s"
            decade_book_counts[decade] += 1

print(f"\nDecades with at least one reviewed book: {len(decade_book_counts)}")
print("Books per decade (all):")
for decade in sorted(decade_book_counts.keys()):
    print(f"  {decade}: {decade_book_counts[decade]} books")

result = {
    'books_with_years': len(books_with_years),
    'books_with_reviews': len(reviews_per_book),
    'decade_distribution': dict(decade_book_counts)
}

print('__RESULT__:')
print(json.dumps(result, indent=2))"}

For complex or non-JSON-serializable types, you must convert them into JSON-compatible forms before printing.

If you cannot proceed, call return_answer with a short explanatory message.

Do not output explanations, reasoning, or any natural language outside of the required tool calls.

QUERY:
Which decade of publication (e.g., 1980s) has the highest average rating among decades with at least 10 distinct books that have been rated? Return the decade with the highest average rating.

DATABASE DESCRIPTION:
You are working with two databases to solve this query.

Here are the descriptions of these two databases:

1. review_database
   - This database is stored in a SQLite database and contains Amazon book review information including ratings, text, helpfulness votes, etc. up to 2023.
   - This database consists of one table:
    - review_database:
      - Fields:
        - rating (float): Rating given by reviewer (1.0-5.0 scale)
        - title (str): Book title
        - text (str): Review text content
        - purchase_id (str): Unique identifier linking to book_id in books_database
        - review_time (str): Timestamp when review was posted
        - helpful_vote (int): Number of helpful votes received
        - verified_purchase (bool): Whether purchase was verified

2. books_database
   - This database is stored in a PostgreSQL database and contains Amazon book information including descriptions, price, details, title, etc. up to 2023.
   - This database consists of one table:
    - books_info:
      - Fields:
        - title (str): Book title
        - subtitle (str): Book subtitle
        - author (str): Book author(s)

        - features (str): Book features (stored as string representation of list/dict)
        - description (str): Book description (stored as string representation of list/dict)
        - price (float): Book price
        - store (str): Store information
        - categories (str): Book categories (stored as string representation of list/dict)
        - details (str): Additional book details
        - book_id (str): Unique book identifier

HINTS: 
- In books_info, the "description", "categories", and "features" content appears to be in list or dictionary format, but they are actually stored as strings in the .sql file.
- The fields "book_id" in books_info and "purchase_id" in review_query.db refer to the same book entities across different tables. While the field names are not exact matches, they can be joined using a fuzzy join approach to solve the query.

messages.content:
The tool query_db was executed successfully. The result is stored under key: var_functions.query_db:16 The result is: [{"rating": "4", "title": "Ha! On me! I thought this was a cookbook!", "text": "Lucky for me it's more than a cookbook.

The story of a modern Daniel Boone, growing up in Michigan, one of three sons of a hunter. Beginning at age eight when he shot a squirrel, to age 13 his first deer, then hunting his way through college, a career as a fur trapper, hunting sheep in Alaska, canoeing the Missouri River for deer and ending in Brooklyn age 37. Brooklyn?

I especially liked that I share his disgust at catch & release fishing - if you fish it, you have to eat it - anything else is sadism (my words).

My brother in law is the cook in the family and I wanted to see if this would be a good gift for him and I struck pay dirt following each chapter are \"Tasting Notes\" where the author speaks of cooking wild game.

I also enjoyed it for the history of the land he has hunted as well as the history of the hunt (I too was a big Daniel Boone fan growing up!).

I think more pictures (everything but dead things) would have made this a keeper.", "review_time": "2012-11-24 18:52:00", "helpful_vote": "0", "verified_purchase": "0", "purchase_id": "purchaseid_186"}, {"rating": "4", "title": "Four Stars", "text": "Not as developed as Stephanie but I like the characters so far.", "review_time": "2015-12-31 13:35:00", "helpful_vote": "0", "verified_purchase": "1", "purchase_id": "purchaseid_191"}, {"rating": "4", "title": "A wonderful adventure in France", "text": "I loved this book all the way until the end. I have recently discovered that the author is intending to release another book, and from what I understand it will continue where this one left off. I am thankful for this, as the way this book ended was definitely a negative for me. I wanted to know more! What happened to the author, did she learn what she came to learn? Did she get the job she was hoping for? So many questions.

From the beginning of Linda's book, it is easy to love her. She is open, honest and definitely has the type of personality you want your heroine to have, whether the book is fiction or reality. I couldn't help but root for Linda throughout her trials with her host family, even when I thought she acted as a bit of a brat herself. Listening to her internal thoughts about what she went through and her desire to achieve her goals made this book feel close to my heart.

I applaud the adventurous spirit of the author and her decision to keep journal entries from that chapter of her life. What great material to have later to inspire a book! While I enjoyed the entire memoir, my favourite part of this book would have to be the author's descriptions of the many places she visited and the people she met along the way. While I think she could definitely have made a better impression on the family she worked for if she had been honest about her lack of French language skills from the beginning, she is a pioneer. Her drive and desire to learn the language from those in the actual country was inspiring. Not many people would have been gutsy enough to do what Linda did.

I also particularly enjoyed the relationship between Linda and Antoine. The Kind heart of the author was apparent in her actions toward the children, even when she struggled with them.

There is some romance, and I appreciated the way the author handled it. While sex scenes are not necessarily automatically offensive, there is something about memoirs that makes me uncomfortable if they are filled with them. This author manages to hint at her experiences without going into too much detail, leaving the reader to decide exactly what happened. Great writing.

When you step into reading this book, you learn a lot about French customs, French cuisine and wine. You also get the opportunity to brush up on your French skills a bit and get to step outside yourself and live an adventure through the eyes of the author. I read this book in one sitting with very few breaks in between and found myself enthralled in the sights, sounds and atmosphere of this memoir.

In the end, I was disappointed that the book ended. I could have kept reading about Linda's experiences without stopping anytime in the foreseeable future. The end of the book leaves many unanswered questions, that I am assuming the author will be answering in her next book. Were this a standalone, I would be very disappointed with the way the book ended, but with the knowledge that she is writing another book to continue with her story, I can't fault this one. One thing is for sure, if you read this book, you will be anxiously awaiting the next.

I would recommend this to anyone who likes books about travel, memoirs or just a good story that came from the recollections and heart of the author. Read it, you will be glad you did. Overall, this was a VERY enjoyable read that gave me many reasons to wish I could drop everything and head off to France!", "review_time": "2013-05-05 10:47:00", "helpful_vote": "1", "verified_purchase": "0", "purchase_id": "purchaseid_190"}]

function.arguments:
{"code": "import json
import re
import pandas as pd

# Load data
books_file = locals().get('var_functions.query_db:28')
reviews_file = locals().get('var_functions.query_db:26')

with open(books_file, 'r') as f:
    books = json.load(f)
with open(reviews_file, 'r') as f:
    reviews = json.load(f)

# Helper to extract numeric ID
extract_number = lambda s: re.search(r'(\d+)', s).group(1) if re.search(r'(\d+)', s) else None

# Process books: add numeric id and year
for book in books:
    book['id_num'] = extract_number(book['book_id'])
    # Extract year from details and subtitle
    text = f"{book.get('details', '')} {book.get('subtitle', '')}"
    # Find 4-digit years between 1900-2023
    years = re.findall(r'(\b(19|20)\d{2}\b)', text)
    year = None
    if years:
        year = int(years[0][0])
    book['year'] = year
    # Compute decade
    if year:
        decade = f"{year//10*10}s"
    else:
        decade = None
    book['decade'] = decade

# Process reviews: add numeric id
for rev in reviews:
    rev['id_num'] = extract_number(rev['purchase_id'])
    # rating already float string
    rev['rating'] = float(rev['rating'])

# Filter books with year not None and rating_number > 0
books_with_year = [b for b in books if b['year'] is not None and int(b['rating_number'])>0]

# Create DataFrames
books_df = pd.DataFrame(books_with_year)
reviews_df = pd.DataFrame(reviews)

# Join on id_num
merged = reviews_df.merge(books_df, left_on='id_num', right_on='id_num', how='inner')

# Group by decade and compute stats
grouped = merged.groupby('decade').agg(
    distinct_books=('book_id', 'nunique'),
    avg_rating=('rating', 'mean')
).reset_index()

# Filter decades with at least 10 distinct books
filtered = grouped[grouped['distinct_books'] >= 10].copy()

# Sort by avg_rating descending
filtered_sorted = filtered.sort_values('avg_rating', ascending=False)

print('Decades with >=10 distinct books:')
print(filtered_sorted.head(10))

# Save to result
result = filtered_sorted.head(1).to_dict(orient='records')[0] if not filtered_sorted.empty else {}
print('Best decade:', result)

# Output
print("__RESULT__:")
print(json.dumps({
    "decade_data": filtered_sorted.to_dict(orient='records'),
    "best_decade": result
}))
"}

{"code": "import json
import re
import pandas as pd

# Load data
books_file = locals().get('var_functions.query_db:28')
reviews_file = locals().get('var_functions.query_db:26')

with open(books_file, 'r') as f:
    books = json.load(f)
with open(reviews_file, 'r') as f:
    reviews = json.load(f)

# Process books: extract year and decade
books_processed = []
for book in books:
    # Extract numeric ID
    id_match = re.search(r'(\d+)', book['book_id'])
    id_num = id_match.group(1) if id_match else None
    
    # Extract year from details/subtitle (look for 4-digit years)
    text = f"{book.get('details', '')} {book.get('subtitle', '')}"
    years = re.findall(r'\b(19|20)\d{2}\b', text)
    year = int(years[0]) if years else None
    
    # Compute decade
    decade = f"{year//10*10}s" if year else None
    
    rating_number = int(book['rating_number'])
    
    books_processed.append({
        'book_id': book['book_id'],
        'id_num': id_num,
        'year': year,
        'decade': decade,
        'rating_number': rating_number
    })

# Process reviews
reviews_processed = []
for rev in reviews:
    id_match = re.search(r'(\d+)', rev['purchase_id'])
    id_num = id_match.group(1) if id_match else None
    rating = float(rev['rating'])
    
    reviews_processed.append({
        'purchase_id': rev['purchase_id'],
        'id_num': id_num,
        'rating': rating
    })

# Filter out None id_num
books_df = pd.DataFrame([b for b in books_processed if b['id_num'] is not None and b['year'] is not None and b['rating_number']>0])
reviews_df = pd.DataFrame([r for r in reviews_processed if r['id_num'] is not None])

print(f"Books with valid data: {len(books_df)}")
print(f"Reviews with valid data: {len(reviews_df)}")

# Merge on id_num
merged = pd.merge(reviews_df, books_df, on='id_num', how='inner')
print(f"Merged rows: {len(merged)}")
print("Sample merged:", merged.head())

# Group by decade
grouped = merged.groupby('decade').agg(
    distinct_books=('book_id', 'nunique'),
    avg_rating=('rating', 'mean')
).reset_index()

# Filter decades with at least 10 distinct books
filtered = grouped[grouped['distinct_books'] >= 10].copy()

# Sort by avg_rating
result_df = filtered.sort_values('avg_rating', ascending=False)

print("Decades with >=10 books:")
print(result_df)

# Get best decade
best = result_df.iloc[0].to_dict() if not result_df.empty else {}

# Output
final = {
    'decades': result_df.to_dict('records'),
    'best_decade': best
}

print("__RESULT__:")
print(json.dumps(final, indent=2))"}

{"code": "import json
import re

# The variables contain file paths
books_path = locals()['var_functions.query_db:28']
reviews_path = locals()['var_functions.query_db:26']

# Load data from files
with open(books_path, 'r') as f:
    books = json.load(f)
with open(reviews_path, 'r') as f:
    reviews = json.load(f)

print("Books loaded:", len(books))
print("Reviews loaded:", len(reviews))

# Extract years from book details/subtitles
books_with_decade = []
for book in books:
    try:
        rating_num = int(book['rating_number'])
        if rating_num == 0:
            continue
            
        # Extract year using regex on details and subtitle
        text = book.get('details', '') + ' ' + book.get('subtitle', '')
        years = re.findall(r'\b(19|20)\d{2}\b', text)
        
        if years:
            year = int(years[0])
            decade = f"{year//10*10}s"
            # Extract book number
            book_num_match = re.search(r'bookid_(\d+)', book['book_id'])
            if book_num_match:
                book_num = book_num_match.group(1)
                books_with_decade.append({
                    'book_id': book['book_id'],
                    'book_num': book_num,
                    'decade': decade,
                    'year': year
                })
    except:
        continue

print("Books with decade extracted:", len(books_with_decade))

# Process reviews - extract purchase numbers
reviews_processed = []
for review in reviews:
    try:
        purchase_num_match = re.search(r'purchaseid_(\d+)', review['purchase_id'])
        if purchase_num_match:
            purchase_num = purchase_num_match.group(1)
            rating = float(review['rating'])
            reviews_processed.append({
                'purchase_id': review['purchase_id'],
                'purchase_num': purchase_num,
                'rating': rating
            })
    except:
        continue

print("Reviews processed:", len(reviews_processed))

# Create dictionaries for lookup
books_dict = {b['book_num']: b for b in books_with_decade}

# Match reviews to books
matched = []
for rev in reviews_processed:
    if rev['purchase_num'] in books_dict:
        book = books_dict[rev['purchase_num']]
        matched.append({
            'book_id': book['book_id'],
            'decade': book['decade'],
            'rating': rev['rating']
        })

print("Matched reviews to books:", len(matched))

# Group by decade
from collections import defaultdict
decades = defaultdict(lambda: {'books': set(), 'ratings': [], 'count': 0})

for item in matched:
    decade = item['decade']
    decades[decade]['books'].add(item['book_id'])
    decades[decade]['ratings'].append(item['rating'])
    decades[decade]['count'] += 1

# Filter decades with at least 10 distinct books
filtered = []
for decade, data in decades.items():
    distinct_books = len(data['books'])
    if distinct_books >= 10:
        avg_rating = sum(data['ratings']) / len(data['ratings'])
        filtered.append({
            'decade': decade,
            'distinct_books': distinct_books,
            'avg_rating': avg_rating,
            'total_ratings': data['count']
        })

# Sort by avg rating
filtered.sort(key=lambda x: x['avg_rating'], reverse=True)

print("Decades with >=10 books:", len(filtered))
for d in filtered[:5]:
    print(d)

# Get best decade
best = filtered[0] if filtered else None

result = {
    'best_decade': best,
    'all_decades': filtered
}

print("__RESULT__:")
print(json.dumps(result, indent=2))"}

{"code": "import json
import re
from collections import defaultdict

# Load data
books_path = locals()['var_functions.query_db:28']
reviews_path = locals()['var_functions.query_db:26']

with open(books_path, 'r') as f:
    books = json.load(f)
with open(reviews_path, 'r') as f:
    reviews = json.load(f)

# Extract book numbers and publication years
books_dict = {}
for book in books:
    # Extract book number
    book_num_match = re.search(r'bookid_(\d+)', book['book_id'])
    if not book_num_match:
        continue
    book_num = book_num_match.group(1)
    
    # Extract year from details/subtitle
    text = book.get('details', '') + ' ' + book.get('subtitle', '')
    years = re.findall(r'\b(19|20)\d{2}\b', text)
    
    if years:
        year = int(years[0])
        decade = f"{year//10*10}s"
        books_dict[book_num] = {
            'book_id': book['book_id'],
            'year': year,
            'decade': decade
        }

# Process reviews
reviews_by_book = defaultdict(list)
for review in reviews:
    # Extract the book number from purchase_id
    purchase_match = re.search(r'purchaseid_(\d+)', review['purchase_id'])
    if not purchase_match:
        continue
    book_num = purchase_match.group(1)
    
    # Extract rating
    try:
        rating = float(review['rating'])
        reviews_by_book[book_num].append(rating)
    except:
        continue

# Group by decade
decade_data = defaultdict(lambda: {'books': set(), 'ratings': []})

for book_num, book_info in books_dict.items():
    if book_num in reviews_by_book:
        decade = book_info['decade']
        decade_data[decade]['books'].add(book_info['book_id'])
        decade_data[decade]['ratings'].extend(reviews_by_book[book_num])

# Filter decades with at least 10 books and compute average ratings
results = []
for decade, data in decade_data.items():
    distinct_books = len(data['books'])
    if distinct_books >= 10:
        avg_rating = sum(data['ratings']) / len(data['ratings'])
        results.append({
            'decade': decade,
            'distinct_books': distinct_books,
            'avg_rating': avg_rating,
            'total_reviews': len(data['ratings'])
        })

# Sort by average rating descending
results.sort(key=lambda x: x['avg_rating'], reverse=True)

print('Found', len(results), 'decades with >=10 distinct books')
for r in results[:5]:
    print(r)

# Create final result
final_result = {
    'best_decade': results[0] if results else None,
    'all_decades': results
}

print("__RESULT__:")
print(json.dumps(final_result, indent=2))"}

# Show ID patterns
print("Sample book IDs (first 20):")
for book in books[:20]:
    print(f"  {book['book_id']}")

print("\nSample review purchase IDs (first 20):")
for review in reviews[:20]:
    print(f"  {review['purchase_id']}")

# Check if purchase_id matches book_id directly
print("\nMatching directly...")
book_ids_set = set(book['book_id'] for book in books)
review_ids_set = set(review['purchase_id'] for review in reviews)

print(f"Unique book IDs: {len(book_ids_set)}")
print(f"Unique review purchase IDs: {len(review_ids_set)}")

# Count exact matches
exact_matches = book_ids_set.intersection(review_ids_set)
print(f"Direct ID matches: {len(exact_matches)}")

if not exact_matches:
    # Try to extract numeric IDs and match those
    print("\nNo direct matches, trying numeric extraction...")
    
    # Extract book numbers
    book_nums = {}
    for book in books:
        match = re.search(r'bookid_(\d+)', book['book_id'])
        if match:
            book_nums[match.group(1)] = book
    
    # Extract review numbers  
    review_nums = {}
    for review in reviews:
        # Try different patterns
        match1 = re.search(r'purchaseid_(\d+)', review['purchase_id'])
        if match1:
            review_nums[match1.group(1)] = review
        else:
            match2 = re.search(r'purchaseid_(\d+)', review['purchase_id'])
            if match2:
                review_nums[match2.group(1)] = review
    
    print(f"Books with extractable numbers: {len(book_nums)}")
    print(f"Reviews with extractable numbers: {len(review_nums)}")
    
    # Count overlapping numbers
    common_nums = set(book_nums.keys()).intersection(review_nums.keys())
    print(f"Overlapping numeric IDs: {len(common_nums)}")
    
    if common_nums:
        print("Sample matches:")

        for num in sample:
            print(f"  Book ID: {book_nums[num]['book_id']}")
            print(f"  Review ID: {review_nums[num]['purchase_id']}")

print("\nDone")"}
